content:
# Leviticus Chapter 21 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 70v–71r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/LEV_021_page_001.jpg`, `LEV_021_page_002.jpg` | **LXX:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 107–108

---

**1** And the LORD said to Moses: "Speak to the priests, the sons of Aaron, and say to them: No one shall make himself unclean for a dead person among his people,

**2** except for his nearest kin: his mother, his father, his son, his daughter, his brother,

**3** and his virgin sister who is near to him, who has had no husband — for her he may make himself unclean.

**4** He shall not make himself unclean as a husband among his people and so profane himself.

**5** They shall not make bald patches on their heads, nor shave off the edges of their beards, nor make any cuts on their body.

**6** They shall be holy to their God and not profane the name of their God, for it is they who offer the LORD's food offerings, the bread of their God; therefore they shall be holy.

**7** They shall not marry a woman who is a prostitute or who has been defiled, nor shall they marry a woman divorced from her husband, for the priest is holy to his God.

**8** You shall consecrate him therefore, for he offers the bread of your God. He shall be holy to you, for I the LORD, who sanctify you, am holy.

**9** And the daughter of any priest, if she profanes herself by whoring, she profanes her father — she shall be burned with fire.

**10** "And the priest who is chief among his brothers, on whose head the anointing oil has been poured and who has been consecrated to wear the garments, shall not let the hair of his head hang loose nor tear his garments.

**11** He shall not go in to any dead body, nor make himself unclean even for his father or for his mother.

**12** He shall not go out of the sanctuary, lest he profane the sanctuary of his God, for the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him: I am the LORD.

**13** And he shall take a wife in her virginity.

**14** A widow, or a divorced woman, or a woman who has been defiled, or a prostitute — these he shall not marry. But he shall take as his wife a virgin from his own people,

**15** so that he may not profane his offspring among his people, for I am the LORD who sanctifies him."

**16** And the LORD spoke to Moses, saying:

**17** "Speak to Aaron, saying: None of your offspring throughout their generations who has a blemish may approach to offer the bread of his God.

**18** For no one who has a blemish shall draw near: a man blind or lame, one who has a disfigured face or a limb too long,

**19** a man who has an injured foot or an injured hand,

**20** or a hunchback or a dwarf or a man with a defect in his sight or an itching disease or scabs or crushed testicles.

**21** No man of the offspring of Aaron the priest who has a blemish shall come near to offer the LORD's food offerings; since he has a blemish, he shall not come near to offer the bread of his God.

**22** He may eat the bread of his God, both the most holy and the holy things,

**23** but he shall not go through the veil or approach the altar, because he has a blemish, that he may not profane my sanctuaries, for I am the LORD who sanctifies them."

**24** So Moses spoke to Aaron and to his sons and to all the people of Israel.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations

**Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 70v–71r:**
Leviticus 21 occupies the latter portion of folio 70v and the opening of folio 71r in the Leningrad Codex. It follows immediately upon the Holiness Code's penalty chapter (ch. 20) and precedes the parallel chapter on priestly food regulations and further blemish disqualifications (ch. 22). Chapter 21 is among the most structurally elegant chapters in the entire Holiness Code: it moves in concentric rings from ordinary priests (vv. 1–9), to the high priest (vv. 10–15), and then addresses the question of physical blemish as a disqualifying condition (vv. 16–24), with each section governed by its own degree of holiness demanded.

The chapter belongs to the Holiness Code (H source, chs. 17–26) but draws on older priestly regulations concerning the bodily integrity required for cultic service. The Masoretic text in B19A is well-preserved with no significant lacunae. The verse-structure is clean and the legal formulas are sharp. The chapter opens with a divine command to Moses to address the priests (v. 1), a unique introduction within Leviticus where most legislation is directed to "the sons of Israel" — here the audience is narrowed to the priestly caste itself, marking the specialized nature of the regulations that follow.

The chapter's organizing principle is the graduated holiness model: every Israelite must be holy (ch. 19–20), but priests must be more holy than the people, and the high priest must be more holy than the priests. Each degree of holiness entails additional restrictions — the ordinary priest may mourn for close relatives but not others; the high priest may not mourn at all. The ordinary priest may marry a non-virgin (so long as she is not a prostitute, defiled woman, or divorcee); the high priest may marry only a virgin. The blemish legislation at the close of the chapter applies the same graduated-holiness logic to bodily condition: physical wholeness is required for access to the altar, though not for eating holy food.

**Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 107–108:**
Leviticus 21 in the Vaticanus spans pages 107 and 108 (imaged as `LEV_021_vaticanus_page_001.jpg` and `LEV_021_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`). Page 107 (pa_0107, Vat.gr.1209_0111) carries the chapter from the opening priestly-mourning regulations through the high priest section, while page 108 (pa_0108, Vat.gr.1209_0112) contains the blemish catalogue and closing. The Vaticanus heading visible at the top of page 107 introduces CAP. VIII (Chapter 8 by the LXX's internal division system — a relic of an older sectional numbering that differs from the modern chapter arrangement). The LXX of chapter 21 follows the MT very closely, though there are important lexical and structural variants that are documented below.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. "Make himself unclean for a dead person" (v. 1) — le-nefesh lo yittamma:**
The verb tame' (Nithpael, "to make oneself unclean, to become impure") takes the preposition le- + nefesh ("for a soul/person/corpse"). Here nefesh is used in its technical sense of "corpse" — a usage attested elsewhere (Num 6:6; 19:13) and common in priestly legislation. The rendering "make himself unclean for a dead person" preserves the forensic-ritual character: the uncleanness in view is not moral but ritual, incurred by contact with or proximity to a corpse. The phrase "among his people" (be-ammav) at the clause's end specifies the social sphere in which the prohibition operates — not a prohibition on all contact with all corpses, but specifically with members of the Israelite community, which is then qualified immediately by the list of permitted kin exceptions (vv. 2–3).

**2. "Nearest kin" (v. 2) — lish'ero ha-qarov elav:**
The phrase lish'ero ha-qarov elav ("for his nearest kin" or "for his flesh that is near to him") uses she'er, a term for close blood-relatives understood as "flesh" — those who share one's own bodily substance. The rendering "nearest kin" (ESV) captures this biological-proximity concept. The list that follows (mother, father, son, daughter, brother, virgin sister) defines the precise circle of the she'er relationship. The logical structure is: the priest may not defile himself for the dead in general (v. 1), except for his closest family members who share his own flesh (vv. 2–3). This exception is more restricted than for ordinary Israelites.

**3. "Virgin sister who is near to him, who has had no husband" (v. 3) — achoto ha-betulah ha-qerovah elav asher lo-haytah le-ish:**
Verse 3 permits the priest to defile himself for an unmarried virgin sister "near to him" (ha-qerovah elav) — one who has not yet been given in marriage and therefore still belongs to the priest's own household. Once she has been married, she enters another man's household and becomes she'ero of her husband rather than her brother. The modifying clause "who has had no husband" (asher lo-haytah le-ish — "who has not been to/belonged to a man") explains the condition that keeps her within the permitted category. The LXX renders this verse particularly carefully, reflecting the same reading. The rendering "virgin sister who is near to him, who has had no husband" follows the MT precisely.

**4. "Profane himself as a husband among his people" (v. 4) — lo yittamma ba'al be-ammav le-hilelo:**
Verse 4 is the most interpretively contested verse in the chapter. The phrase ba'al be-ammav is literally "as a husband/master among his people." Three main interpretations have been offered: (a) the priest may not defile himself for his wife's relatives (she belongs to another family, not his she'er), since as a "husband" she is not his nearest kin; (b) the priest who is a "leader/master" (ba'al as ruler) among his people may not defile himself at all beyond the permitted circle; (c) a general prohibition against defiling himself except as allowed in vv. 2–3. The rendering "He shall not make himself unclean as a husband among his people and so profane himself" follows the reading that takes ba'al in the sense of "husband" — the priest may not extend the permitted mourning defilements to his wife's kin, since his wife belongs to him by marriage but her blood-relatives are not his she'er. This follows the reading of most major commentators and the ESV/NASB tradition.

**5. "Bald patches on their heads" / "shave off the edges of their beards" / "make any cuts on their body" (v. 5) — qorchah be-ro'sham / pe'at zeqanam / bisaram lo yisrotu:**
Verse 5 prohibits three mourning practices associated with surrounding cultures: (a) qorchah — a bald patch cut or shaved on the head as a mourning rite (cf. Deut 14:1; Jer 16:6; Ezek 27:31; Amos 8:10); (b) shaving "the edges of their beards" (pe'at zeqanam — parallel to the prohibition in 19:27); (c) cutting incisions into the body (lo yisrotu saretet bisaram — "they shall not cut cuts in their flesh," a mourning practice attested in Ugaritic texts and condemned repeatedly in the OT). These three prohibited practices are all associated with Near Eastern mourning customs and are here forbidden to the priests because their priestly vocation must not be compromised by pagan mourning rites. The rendering "make bald patches," "shave off the edges of their beards," and "make any cuts on their body" follows the ESV/NASB tradition.

**6. "The bread of their God" (v. 6) — lechem Eloheihem:**
The phrase lechem Eloheihem ("the bread of their God") appears four times in chapter 21 (vv. 6, 8, 17, 21) and once more in 22:25. It is a specialized technical term for the sacrificial offerings brought on the altar — understood as the "food" of YHWH in an analogical sense (cf. Num 28:2: "My offering, the food for my food offerings, my pleasing aroma"). The language is anthropomorphic but not intended literally; rather, it reflects the sacrificial system's conceptual framework in which YHWH as host receives the tribute of his people at his table (the altar). "The bread of their God" (ESV, NASB, KJV) preserves the technical character of the phrase. Modern alternatives ("the food offerings of their God," "the offerings made to their God") correctly identify the referent but sacrifice the evocative biblical idiom.

**7. "Who is chief among his brothers" (v. 10) — ha-gadol me-echav:**
The high priest is designated here not by the full title kohen ha-gadol (as in 21:10 some manuscripts) but by the descriptor ha-gadol me-echav — "the great one among his brothers," i.e., the one who surpasses his fellow priests in rank and function. This relational description emphasizes that the high priest is first among equals in the priestly family, not a categorically different type of person. The rendering "the priest who is chief among his brothers" follows the ESV and captures both the superlative greatness and the fraternal relationship implied.

**8. "On whose head the anointing oil has been poured" (v. 10) — asher yutaq al-rosho shemen ha-mishchah:**
The Hophal of yatsaq ("to pour") — yutaq — is the passive form: "upon whose head has been poured the anointing oil." The high priest's unique status is marked by the anointing with the special anointing oil (shemen ha-mishchah) prepared according to the formula in Exodus 30:23–33. The oil was poured (yatsaq) rather than simply smeared, suggesting a more lavish application. The rendering "on whose head the anointing oil has been poured" follows the MT's passive form precisely.

**9. "Let the hair of his head hang loose" (v. 10) — et-rosho lo yifra':**
The verb para' (Qal, "to let loose, dishevel, uncover") applied to the hair indicates the disheveled appearance of mourning — uncut or unbound hair allowed to hang loose as an expression of grief (cf. Lev 10:6, where Moses commands Aaron and his remaining sons not to let their hair hang loose after the death of Nadab and Abihu). The high priest's hair must at all times be properly dressed; mourning disarray is prohibited. "Shall not let the hair of his head hang loose" (ESV) preserves the specific idiom.

**10. "The crown of the anointing oil" (v. 12) — nezer shemen mishchat Elohav:**
The term nezer (נֵזֶר) is the consecration-crown or diadem, used for both the royal crown and the Nazirite's consecrated status (Num 6:7). Here it denotes the high priest's anointed consecration — the "crown" of the anointing oil is upon him, meaning his anointing constitutes a crown of consecration that sets him apart absolutely. This is one of the most theologically dense phrases in Leviticus 21: the high priest's anointing functions as a crown (royal dignity), a consecration (priestly holiness), and a bond to YHWH (he may not break it by leaving the sanctuary for any reason, even family death). The rendering "the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him" (ESV: "the consecration of the anointing oil of his God") preserves nezer as "crown" to maintain the coronation-consecration lexical link.

**11. "He shall not go out of the sanctuary" (v. 12) — u-min ha-miqdash lo yetse':**
The high priest's restriction from departing the sanctuary during the mourning period (when a family member dies) goes further than the ordinary priest's restriction, which only prohibits contact with the corpse. The high priest may not even leave the sacred precincts. This absolute restriction serves a structural purpose: his continuous presence in the sanctuary is a cultic requirement, not merely a personal preference. The sanctuary's holiness depends on the high priest's uninterrupted ritual purity. The rendering "He shall not go out of the sanctuary" is straightforward.

**12. "A virgin from his own people" (v. 14) — betulah me-ammav yiqach ishah:**
The high priest's marriage restriction is the strictest in the priestly code: he may only marry a betulah ("virgin," one who has not had sexual intercourse) from his own people (me-ammav). The four prohibited categories listed in v. 14 (widow, divorced woman, defiled woman, prostitute) precisely mirror the ordinary priest's prohibitions (v. 7) except that the widow prohibition is added: even a chaste widow is barred from the high priest's household, since a widow has had intercourse with her first husband. The reason is v. 15: the high priest must not "profane his seed among his people" — his lineage must be of the highest possible purity. The rendering "a virgin from his own people" (ESV) preserves both the status requirement (betulah) and the ethnic-communal restriction (me-ammav).

**13. "That he may not profane his offspring" (v. 15) — ve-lo yechalel zar'o be-ammav:**
The verb chalal (Piel, "to profane, desecrate") appears throughout ch. 21 as the key term for the degradation of priestly holiness. In v. 15 it takes "his offspring" (zar'o) as its object: improper marriage would profane the high priest's seed, potentially producing children of compromised priestly status. The concern is not merely hereditary but theo-cultic: the high priestly lineage must be unimpeachable because the high priest alone enters the Most Holy Place on Yom Kippur. The rendering "that he may not profane his offspring among his people" preserves the Piel force and the social-communal context.

**14. "Has a blemish" — mum:**
The term mum (מוּם, "blemish, defect") is the key term of vv. 17–24, occurring four times (vv. 17, 18, 21, 23). It denotes a physical defect or imperfection that disqualifies a priest from altar service, paralleling the requirement that sacrificial animals also be "without blemish" (tamim, without mum). The parallel is not incidental: the priestly theology of Leviticus understands the priest as himself a kind of representative offering — his body, standing before the altar, must meet the same standards as the animals offered upon it. The rendering "blemish" (ESV, NASB, KJV) is the standard formal equivalent, preferred over "defect" (NIV) for its stronger cultic connotation.

**15. The blemish catalogue (vv. 18–20):**
The list of disqualifying blemishes in vv. 18–20 presents lexical challenges, some of which remain incompletely resolved:

- **ivver** (v. 18): "blind" — well-attested; no dispute.
- **piseach** (v. 18): "lame" — standard rendering; cf. 2 Sam 5:6, 8.
- **charum** (v. 18): "disfigured face" (ESV: "mutilated face") — the precise meaning is disputed; the root may indicate a split nose, a flattened nose, or more generally any facial disfigurement. LOGOS renders "disfigured face" following ESV.
- **sarua'** (v. 18): "a limb too long" — literally "extended/spread," i.e., one limb disproportionately elongated relative to the other. Some read this as "one with an extra limb" (ESV: "limb too long"); others as a skin condition. LOGOS follows "a limb too long" (ESV).
- **shever regel o shever yad** (v. 19): "an injured foot or an injured hand" — shever is literally "break," here a broken or deformed limb (whether from injury or congenital cause).
- **gibben** (v. 20): "hunchback" — well-attested in ancient Hebrew lexicography; LXX κυρτός ("bent, hump-backed").
- **dak** (v. 20): "dwarf" — the meaning is debated; dak may refer to a very thin/emaciated person, a dwarf, or one with wasting disease. LOGOS renders "dwarf" following ESV/NASB and the LXX μικρόφθαλμος (which actually renders the next term, creating a shift in LXX ordering).
- **tevel be-eino** (v. 20): "a defect in his sight" — literally "confusion/blemish in his eye"; probably referring to a diseased or opaque eye (cataract, opacity).
- **garav** (v. 20): "itching disease" (ESV) — a skin condition; Talmudic tradition associates this with a weeping or oozing skin lesion.
- **yalefet** (v. 20): "scabs" — another skin condition, possibly a chronic scabrous eruption.
- **meruach ashekh** (v. 20): "crushed testicles" — a genital injury resulting in castration or dysfunction. This condition disqualifies from priestly altar service (as it does from the congregation in Deut 23:1). The rendering "crushed testicles" follows the literal MT reading and the ESV/NASB tradition; euphemisms are not employed in formal translation.

**16. "The bread of his God, both the most holy and the holy things" (v. 22) — lechem Elohav mi-qodesh ha-qedashim u-min ha-qedashim yokhel:**
The blemished priest retains one privilege: he may eat the sacred food (lechem Elohav — "the bread of his God"), specifically both the "most holy things" (qodesh ha-qedashim — the grade-one sacred food: sin offering meat, guilt offering meat, grain offerings; Lev 2:3; 6:25–29; 7:1) and the "holy things" (qedashim — the grade-two sacred food: peace offering portions, first-fruits, etc.). His bodily blemish removes him from altar ministry but not from the priestly food-sharing community. This is a significant concession: the blemished priest is not expelled from the priesthood — only from its specific cultic function at the altar and veil. The rendering "the bread of his God, both the most holy and the holy things" preserves the technical two-tier distinction.

**17. "The veil" and "the altar" (v. 23) — parokhet / mizbe'ach:**
Verse 23 specifies the two things from which the blemished priest is barred: the parokhet ("the veil" — the curtain separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place; cf. Exod 26:31–33) and the mizbe'ach ("the altar" — the altar of burnt offering in the outer court). The parokhet-prohibition bars the blemished priest from the inner sanctuary; the mizbe'ach-prohibition bars him from the primary locus of sacrificial activity in the outer court. Both prohibitions arise from the same rationale: bodily wholeness is required for direct approach to the holy places. "The veil" is the standard ESV/NASB rendering for parokhet throughout Leviticus and Exodus.

**18. "That he may not profane my sanctuaries" (v. 23) — ve-lo yechalel et-miqdashay:**
The plural miqdashay ("my sanctuaries") encompasses all the sacred spaces of the tabernacle complex — the outer court, the Holy Place, and the Most Holy Place. The singular form elsewhere refers to the tabernacle as a whole; the plural here may emphasize the totality of the sacred space from which the blemished priest's presence at the altar would introduce profanation. The theological reasoning is that the blemish-prohibition for priests parallels the blemish-prohibition for sacrificial animals: both must be tamim (whole, complete) to stand before YHWH in his sanctuary.

**19. "I am the LORD who sanctifies them" (v. 23) — ani YHWH meqadsham:**
The sanctification formula meqadsham ("who sanctifies them") closes the blemish section, as a similar formula (mekadeshkhem — "who sanctifies you") closes earlier sections (v. 8; cf. 20:8; 21:15; 22:9, 16, 32). The formula functions as YHWH's self-identification as the active agent of all holiness in the priestly system: the priests do not generate their own holiness but receive it from YHWH who sanctifies them. The rendering "I am the LORD who sanctifies them" follows the ESV/NASB tradition.

**20. "So Moses spoke to Aaron and to his sons and to all the people of Israel" (v. 24):**
The closing verse is a Mosaic transmission formula confirming that the entirety of the chapter's legislation was duly communicated to its intended audiences: Aaron (the high priest), his sons (the ordinary priests), and the full congregation. The inclusion of "all the people of Israel" (kol bney Yisra'el — "all the sons of Israel") at the close of what was primarily addressed to priests is significant: the people as a whole needed to know the priestly holiness standards, since they were responsible for upholding them (cf. v. 8: "You shall consecrate him, therefore" — directed to the congregation, not to the priests themselves). The rendering is straightforward.

---

### LXX Vaticanus Variants

| Verse | MT (Hebrew) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Note |
|-------|-------------|-----------------|------|
| 1 | le-nefesh lo yittamma ("he shall not make himself unclean for a dead person") | οὐκ ἐπὶ ψυχῇ τεθνηκυίᾳ μιανθήσεται ("he shall not defile himself for a dead soul") | LXX: ψυχή + τεθνηκυίᾳ (dead soul/person) — adds the participle "dead" to make explicit what MT implies; functionally equivalent |
| 2 | lish'ero ha-qarov elav ("for his nearest kin") | ἀλλ' ἢ ἐπὶ τοῖς οἰκείοις τοῖς ἐγγίζουσιν αὐτῷ ("but only for his household members who are near to him") | LXX: οἰκεῖος ("household member, relative") for she'er ("flesh, kin"); ἐγγίζω ("draw near") for qarov ("near"); the LXX gives a relational rather than bodily (flesh) framing |
| 3 | achoto ha-betulah ha-qerovah elav asher lo-haytah le-ish ("his virgin sister near to him who has had no husband") | τὴν ἀδελφὴν αὐτοῦ τὴν παρθένον τὴν σύνεγγυς αὐτοῦ τὴν μὴ ἐκδεδομένην ἀνδρί ("his virgin sister who is near to him, the one not given to a man") | LXX: ἐκδίδωμι ("to give out, to give in marriage") for haytah le-ish ("belonged to a man"); the LXX frames this as a formal betrothal-marriage act (giving in marriage) |
| 4 | ba'al be-ammav le-hilelo ("as a husband among his people and so profane himself") | οὐ μιανθήσεται ἐξάπινα ἐν τῷ λαῷ αὐτοῦ ὥστε βεβηλωθῆναι αὐτόν ("he shall not suddenly defile himself among his people so as to be profaned") | LXX: ἐξάπινα ("suddenly, unexpectedly") — the LXX appears to read the verse very differently; "suddenly" may reflect a different Vorlage or a creative rendering; the ba'al ("husband/master") element is not reflected at all |
| 5 | lo yiqrechu qorchah be-ro'sham ("they shall not make bald patches on their heads") | καὶ κεφαλὴν οὐ ξυρηθήσονται ("and they shall not be shaved on the head") | LXX: ξυράω ("to shave") rather than the MT's specific term for making a bald patch (qorchah); LXX generalizes the mourning-hair prohibition to any head-shaving |
| 5 | ve-lo yegallechu pe'at zeqanam ("nor shave off the edges of their beards") | καὶ πώγωνα οὐ ξυρήσονται ("nor shall they shave their beard") | LXX: drops the "edges" (pe'at) specification and simply prohibits beard-shaving; a slight simplification of the MT |
| 7 | zonah va-chalalah lo yiqachu ("a prostitute or who has been defiled they shall not marry") | γυναῖκα πόρνην ἢ βεβηλωμένην οὐ λήμψονται ("a prostitute woman or one who has been profaned they shall not take") | LXX: βεβηλόω ("to profane") for chalalah ("defiled/profaned"); exact semantic equivalent; πόρνη for zonah as standard |
| 10 | et-rosho lo yifra' ("shall not let his hair hang loose") | τὴν κεφαλὴν οὐκ ἀποκιδαρώσει ("shall not uncover/bare his head") | LXX: ἀποκιδαρόω (a rare verb, possibly "to remove the mitre/headgear") — a very different reading from the MT's "loose hair"; the LXX may reflect a tradition understanding para' as removing the priestly headgear |
| 10 | ve-bigdav lo yiqra' ("nor tear his garments") | καὶ τὰ ἱμάτια οὐ διαρρήξει ("and shall not tear his garments") | LXX: διαρρήγνυμι ("to tear apart") for qara' ("to tear"); precise equivalent |
| 12 | nezer shemen mishchat Elohav alav ("the crown of the anointing oil of his God is upon him") | ὅτι τὸ ἅγιον ἔλαιον τὸ χριστὸν τοῦ θεοῦ ἐπ' αὐτῷ ("because the holy anointing oil of God is upon him") | LXX: renders nezer as "holy" (ἅγιον) rather than "crown/consecration" — the nezer-as-crown concept is not captured; the LXX emphasizes the sacredness of the oil rather than its function as a crown of consecration |
| 13 | betulah me-ammav yiqach ishah ("a virgin from his own people he shall take as wife") | παρθένον ἐκ τοῦ γένους αὐτοῦ λήμψεται γυναῖκα ("a virgin from his own clan he shall take as wife") | LXX: γένος ("clan, race, stock") for ammav ("his people"); a slight narrowing — from the broad "his people" to the specific "his clan/lineage" |
| 17 | kol-ish me-zar'akha le-dorotam asher yihyeh bo mum ("any man from your offspring throughout their generations who has a blemish") | ἄνθρωπος ἐξ αὐτῶν ᾧ ἂν ᾖ ἐν αὐτῷ μῶμος | LXX: μῶμος for mum; a direct loanword borrowing or exact lexical match; LXX drops the "throughout their generations" phrase |
| 18 | charum ("disfigured face") | κολοβόρριν ("short-nosed, snub-nosed") | LXX: κολοβόρριν is a very specific term for a mutilated or shortened nose; confirms the facial-disfigurement reading of charum but specifies the nose |
| 18 | sarua' ("a limb too long") | ὠτότμητος ("one with a cut ear") | LXX: ὠτότμητος is a remarkably divergent reading — "one with a cut ear" versus MT's "one with a limb too long"; this is one of the most striking MT/LXX divergences in the catalogue, possibly reflecting a different Vorlage or a lexical confusion |
| 20 | gibben ("hunchback") | κυρτός ("bent, hump-backed") | LXX: κυρτός — precise semantic equivalent |
| 20 | dak ("dwarf") | ψωριῶν ("one with mange/scab-disease") | LXX: a radically different reading; the LXX appears to understand dak as a skin condition rather than a size-related condition; this may reflect an alternative tradition or a different manuscript reading |
| 20 | tevel be-eino ("a defect in his sight") | μικρόφθαλμος ("small-eyed, having small eyes") | LXX: μικρόφθαλμος — "having small eyes"; the MT's more general "defect in his eye" is rendered as a specific small-eye condition |
| 20 | garav ("itching disease") | ψώρᾳ ἀγρίᾳ ("wild/severe mange") | LXX: ψώρα ἀγρία — "wild mange"; adds the intensifier "wild/severe" |
| 20 | yalefet ("scabs") | λειχῆνι ("lichen scabiosa, scaly skin disease") | LXX: a specific dermatological term; confirms the skin-disease reading |
| 20 | meruach ashekh ("crushed testicles") | μονόρχης ("one with a single testicle") | LXX: μονόρχης ("monorchid, having only one testicle") — a somewhat different condition from the MT's "crushed testicles"; the LXX may reflect a different understanding of the Hebrew or a different Vorlage |
| 23 | ha-parokhet ("the veil") | τὸ καταπέτασμα ("the curtain/veil") | LXX: καταπέτασμα is the standard LXX rendering for parokhet; this is the same word used in the NT for the Temple veil that was torn at the crucifixion (Matt 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45) |
| 24 | va-yedabber Moshe el-Aharon ve-el-banav ve-el kol bney Yisra'el ("So Moses spoke to Aaron and to his sons and to all the sons of Israel") | καὶ ἐλάλησεν Μωσῆς πρὸς Ααρων καὶ πρὸς τοὺς υἱοὺς αὐτοῦ καὶ πρὸς πάντας υἱοὺς Ισραηλ | LXX: precise structural equivalent; Μωσῆς/Ααρων as standard transliterations |

---

### Consistent Term Renderings

| Hebrew Term | LOGOS Rendering | Notes |
|-------------|-----------------|-------|
| YHWH | the LORD | Small capitals; standard throughout |
| kohen / kohanim | priest / priests | Standard; no alternative |
| kohen ha-gadol / ha-gadol me-echav | high priest / the priest who is chief among his brothers | v. 10; the full title is implied; the relational description preserved |
| mum | blemish | vv. 17, 18, 21, 23; standard formal term; parallels animal-sacrifice terminology |
| nefesh (= corpse) | dead person | v. 1; nefesh in its technical priestly-legislation sense of "corpse" |
| she'er | nearest kin | v. 2; "flesh" = blood relatives; rendered by relationship rather than body-part |
| betulah | virgin | vv. 3, 13, 14; woman who has not had sexual intercourse |
| lechem Elohav | the bread of his God | vv. 6, 8, 17, 21; technical term for altar offerings as YHWH's food |
| qorchah | bald patches | v. 5; mourning-shaving of the head |
| pe'at zeqanam | the edges of their beards | v. 5; cf. 19:27 |
| saretet | cuts | v. 5; incisions/gashes in the body; mourning rite |
| chalal (Piel) | profane | vv. 4, 6, 9, 12, 15, 23; desecrate, render unholy; key term of ch. 21 |
| nezer shemen mishchah | crown of the anointing oil | v. 12; priestly consecration as crown/diadem |
| para' (hair) | let hang loose | v. 10; disheveled mourning hair |
| zonah | prostitute | v. 7, 14; female sex-worker or cult prostitute |
| chalalah | woman who has been defiled | vv. 7, 14; lit. "profaned woman" — woman whose sexual history disqualifies |
| gerushah | divorced woman | vv. 7, 14; one sent away by her husband |
| almanah | widow | v. 14; restricted for high priest but not ordinary priest |
| qodesh ha-qedashim | most holy things | v. 22; grade-one sacred food |
| qedashim | holy things | v. 22; grade-two sacred food |
| parokhet | the veil | v. 23; the curtain of the sanctuary |
| mizbe'ach | the altar | v. 23; the altar of burnt offering |
| miqdashay | my sanctuaries | v. 23; plural: all the sacred spaces of the tabernacle complex |
| meqadesh / meqadsham | who sanctifies / who sanctifies them | vv. 8, 15, 23; YHWH as the active agent of priestly holiness |
| bney Yisra'el | people of Israel | v. 24; the full Israelite congregation |

---

— LOGOS
